paragraphs.text: Two angels come to Sodom, draw near the gates at which Lot sat to receive strangers entering the city. “When Lot saw, he rose up to meet them,” as he usually met strangers. But when he drew near them, in one of the two who had come he saw the same as Abraham had seen in one of the three, “and bowed himself with his face toward the ground” (Gen 19:1). It is likely that the angels who came to the Sodomites had a beautiful appearance, for the words “I will go down now, and see” (Gen 18:21) mean the same as “going down, I will test them.” If the Sodomites, beholding their face, had not gone into madness, then though they would not have received forgiveness of their former sins, neither would they have been subjected to the punishment that afterward overtook them.

Lot hastens to bring the strangers into his house before the Sodomites gather and are scandalized. But the strangers delay, giving the Sodomites time to come and be tested. They had not refused to enter Abraham’s house, because they had come not to tempt him but to render reward to one already tested. But they came to Sodom to test the Sodomites; therefore to Lot, who urged them to enter his house, they say: “Nay, but we will abide in the street all night” (Gen 19:2).

When Lot “pressed upon them… and they entered into his house” (Gen 19:3), and had tasted the meal Lot offered, but had not yet lain down to rest, then “the men of the city, the Sodomites, compassed the house” (Gen 19:4), and say to Lot: “Where are the men who came in to thee this night? bring them out unto us, that we may know them” (Gen 19:5). See, the strangers came not by day, when the Sodomites might see their beauty and be tempted, but by night, when darkness hid that beauty from their sight, so that the temptation might not be so strong. But this too was of no benefit to the Sodomites. By night as by day they were preparing perdition for their souls. Lot tries to persuade the Sodomites, but they are not persuaded; he offers them his two daughters, but they do not accept, and with threats they say: “We will deal worse with thee than with them… and they pressed sore to break the door” (Gen 19:9). Then the strangers “pulled Lot into the house to them,” and the Sodomites who were “at the door of the house, they smote with blindness” (Gen 19:10–11). But not even this stopped the madness of the Sodomites, for afterward they still kept searching for the door. “And the men said unto Lot: Hast thou here any besides? son-in-law, and thy sons, and thy daughters, and whomsoever thou hast in the city, bring them out of this place: for we will destroy this place” (Gen 19:12–13). By “sons” are meant the sons-in-law who wished to take his daughters in marriage. “And Lot went out, and spoke unto his sons-in-law” (Gen 19:14). The Sodomites did not notice how he went out of the house and how he returned. Even when Lot returned, mocked by his sons-in-law, and “the angels laid hold upon his hand, and upon the hand of his wife, and upon the hand of his two daughters, and brought him forth” (Gen 19:16), even then the Sodomites did not see how they passed in a crowd through their midst.

Since the women in Sodom had not been put to the test, they were tested by the commandment given to them after coming out of Sodom. When Lot begins to ask that Zoar be spared, that he might enter it, for it was not far off, then the angel says to him in answer: “Behold, I have accepted thee concerning this thing also, that I will not overthrow the city” (Gen 19:21). This shall be given thee for the dishonor of both thy daughters. When Lot entered Zoar, “the Lord rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire from the Lord out of heaven” (Gen 19:24) — that is, the angel in whom the Lord appeared, from the Lord who is in the heavens, brought down brimstone and fire on Sodom. But Lot’s wife transgressed the commandment given her for testing for a short time, “and became a pillar of salt” (Gen 19:26), and by this she made the temptation of Lot and his two daughters yet greater, but even after this they did not bend so far as to transgress the commandment of the angel.

Lot’s daughters, fearing to dwell in the deserted city, began to beg their father to flee to a mountain. And since they thought that the fiery flood had destroyed the whole world, as in Noah’s time the whole world had been destroyed by a water flood, “the elder said to the younger: Our father is old, and there is not a man in the earth to come in unto us… Let us make our father drink wine… and let us preserve seed of our father, and there shall come from us a third world, as from Noah’s house came the second, and from Adam and Eve the first” (Gen 19:31, 32). They had no want of wine, because everything in Zoar had come into their possession. The inhabitants of Zoar had vanished, for when the angel said to Lot: “Behold, I have accepted thee concerning this thing also, that I will not overthrow the city,” Zoar swallowed up its inhabitants, leaving their possessions. Their inhabitants it swallowed up, to appease the Righteous One whom they had angered by their deeds; their possessions it left for righteous Lot, that he might be consoled, having lost all he had had in Sodom. Lot’s daughters invented pretexts and said: “We are afraid to sleep; phantoms terrify us; before us stands our mother, turned into a pillar of salt; the burning Sodomites appear to our eyes; the cries of women calling out of the fire ring in our ears; we see, as it were before us, children suffering in the flames; therefore, father, do not sleep, but to soothe your daughters take pleasure in wine, that you may pass the night watching, which will deliver us from these horrors.” But when they observed that Lot had lost his reason from wine and a deep sleep had taken hold of his members, then the elder went in and took the seed of the sleeping man, “and he was not aware” (Gen 19:33). Then the elder sister, seeing that what she had contrived had been done, began also to draw her younger sister to become, for a time, a wife, but then forever to remain a virgin; and the younger, persuaded by her sister, went in and came out, “and he was not aware” (Gen 19:35).

“And the elder bore a son, and called his name Moab” (Gen 19:37), and he became the founder of a great nation, as a son of Lot. “The younger also bore a son, and called his name Ben-ammi (Ammon or Bar-ammi)” (Gen 19:38), that is, “son of my people,” because the son of my father. So two sons were given — corresponding to the two transgressions. Two sons were given for two peoples, but for the sake of two angels the two transgressions were forgiven. Lot’s daughters afterward lived neither with Lot, because he is their father, nor with others, although there were suitors for them. But since they had hastened to do what should not have been done, they abstained also from what was permitted. By the abstinence that followed, they probably blotted out the earlier haste.
